text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending]
FELLOW: [Fellow]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for oncology follow-up in the setting of confirmed enzalutamide resistance based on three consecutive rising PSA values over the past 4 months, now with new imaging findings of progressive osseous and nodal disease. He reports worsening left hip pain and new onset left shoulder pain.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is a retired steelworker with a 45 pack-year smoking history (quit at age 62) who was originally diagnosed approximately 4 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC). At diagnosis, he had Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 acinar adenocarcinoma with cT3b disease (bilateral seminal vesicle invasion), N1 pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator node 1.8 cm), and M1b osseous metastases involving 8 discrete sites on bone scan (L2, T9, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, and multiple rib lesions). PSA at diagnosis was 187 ng/mL. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*, nonsense) was identified and confirmed from peripheral blood at that time. Tumor sequencing also revealed concurrent TP53 p.R248Q, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 loss, and CDK12 frameshift mutation.

He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID for mHSPC. He had an excellent initial response, with PSA declining from 187 to a nadir of 1.1 ng/mL over approximately 14 months, and significant improvement in osseous disease on restaging imaging at 6 months. He maintained this response for a prolonged period.

Approximately 2 years after initiating first-line therapy, PSA began to rise. By the time of confirmed castration resistance (PSA 8.7 ng/mL, testosterone confirmed at castrate 22 ng/dL, two consecutive rises over 3 months), restaging imaging showed progression with new lesions on bone scan (left femoral head, T12 vertebral body) and enlargement of pelvic lymph nodes. Abiraterone was discontinued and enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily was initiated as second-line hormonal therapy, with ADT maintained.

Enzalutamide provided a robust initial PSA response, with PSA declining from 8.7 to a nadir of 1.4 ng/mL at 6 months (83.9% decline). Restaging imaging at that time showed improvement in osseous disease and regression of nodal disease. However, PSA has now risen on three consecutive measurements over 4 months:
  - PSA ~4.3 ng/mL (2 months ago)
  - PSA ~9.7 ng/mL (6 weeks ago)
  - PSA 14.3 ng/mL (today)

Today's restaging bone scan and CT confirm disease progression. Bone scan reveals multiple new lesions (left iliac crest x2, right proximal humerus, sacrum) with progression at the right femoral neck, bringing total osseous disease burden to approximately 17 lesions. CT demonstrates significant enlargement of the right obturator node (now 2.1 cm), as well as new retroperitoneal adenopathy including a right para-aortic node at 1.4 cm and aortocaval node at 1.1 cm. No visceral (hepatic or pulmonary) metastases identified on current CT.

With respect to symptoms, Mr. Kowalski reports new left hip pain over the past 3-4 weeks, which he rates 4/10 at rest and up to 6/10 with ambulation. He is walking with a mild antalgic gait. He also notes new aching in the left shoulder, 2-3/10, which he attributes to a new bone lesion in the proximal humerus seen on imaging today. His previously treated right hip/femoral neck pain is well controlled. He reports fatigue that has been worsening progressively, limiting his activities. He is spending more than half of his waking hours resting. His appetite has declined somewhat, though he has not had frank anorexia. Weight loss of approximately 6 pounds over the past 3 months. He denies fever, chills, dyspnea, chest pain, hematuria, urinary obstruction, bowel changes, or neurological symptoms including saddle anesthesia, weakness, or bowel/bladder incontinence.

Notably, he has known germline BRCA2 p.E1308* and has been counseled regarding PARP inhibitor eligibility. Given this mutation and the clinical trajectory of enzalutamide resistance, olaparib has been discussed at prior visits as the next planned treatment step.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: fatigue (worsening), decreased appetite, weight loss (~6 lbs in 3 months), left hip pain (new, 4-6/10), left shoulder pain (new, 2-3/10), ongoing hot flashes from ADT, mild cognitive slowing (stable per patient's report, pre-existing on enzalutamide).
Negative: no fever, no night sweats, no dyspnea at rest, no chest pain, no palpitations, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal pain, no jaundice, no lower extremity edema, no hematuria, no urinary retention, no bowel obstruction, no neurological deficits, no falls, no saddle anesthesia.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND ONCOLOGIC HISTORY:
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone and lymph node metastases, now progressing on enzalutamide.
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*, confirmed).
3. Prior systemic therapies:
   a. ADT (leuprolide acetate) + abiraterone/prednisone x ~2 years (mHSPC first-line, discontinued at CRPC progression)
   b. Enzalutamide 160 mg daily x ~9 months (mCRPC second-line, now discontinued for progression)
4. Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (bone-directed therapy, dose-adjusted for CKD, ongoing).
5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - on metformin.
6. Hypertension - on amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 20 mg daily (doses adjusted during enzalutamide course for blood pressure elevation).
7. Mild-to-moderate chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, now 1.6 mg/dL, eGFR ~42; mild worsening over course of treatment).
8. Anemia of chronic disease (mild, hemoglobin 9.6 mg/dL on most recent CBC).

FAMILY HISTORY:
Brother: pancreatic cancer, age 58. Father: prostate cancer, age 74. BRCA2 germline testing offered to relatives (counseling previously completed).

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired steelworker. Married, lives with wife. Two adult children who are engaged in his care. 45 pack-year smoking history, quit age 62. Occasional alcohol in past, none currently. No illicit drug use. Lives in a single-story home. Currently ambulatory but limiting activity due to pain and fatigue.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA.

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (ADT, ongoing)
2. Enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily (being DISCONTINUED today - see plan)
3. Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (last dose administered 6 weeks ago; next due in 6 weeks)
4. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
5. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily
6. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily
7. Acetaminophen 1000 mg orally TID (as needed for bone pain)
8. Extended-release oxycodone 10 mg orally twice daily (added at prior visit for worsening bone pain)
9. Ondansetron 8 mg orally PRN nausea
10. Prednisone 5 mg orally daily (continued from initial abiraterone regimen, maintained for adrenal suppression)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vitals: Blood pressure 144/88 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 degrees C, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 176 lbs (decreased from 182 lbs 3 months ago, and from 191 lbs at enzalutamide start), height 5'10".

General: Alert and oriented x3. Elderly male appearing somewhat fatigued, in no acute distress at rest. Mild temporal wasting appreciated.

HEENT: Anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. No abdominal masses. Bowel sounds active.

Musculoskeletal / Neurological: Antalgic gait favoring the left side. Tenderness to palpation over the left greater trochanter region and left iliac crest. Tenderness over the left proximal humerus on direct palpation. No tenderness to percussion over the thoracic or lumbar spine. Lower extremity strength: 4/5 bilaterally in hip flexors, 5/5 at knee extension. No focal neurological deficits. No signs of cord compression.

Skin: No rashes. Prior radiation field on right proximal thigh/femoral neck with residual mild hyperpigmentation, well healed.

Digital rectal exam: Not performed at this visit.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):
PSA: 14.3 ng/mL (nadir 1.4 ng/mL; rising x3 values over 4 months)
Testosterone: 21 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
CBC: WBC 5.4 K/uL, ANC 3.2 K/uL, hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), MCV 89 fL, platelets 194,000/uL
Comprehensive metabolic panel: sodium 138, potassium 4.1, CO2 24, BUN 28, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR ~42, mild worsening from baseline 1.4), glucose 112, albumin 3.4 g/dL, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, AST 34 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 198 U/L (elevated, reflects osseous disease burden)
LDH: 267 U/L (elevated)
HbA1c: 7.2% (diabetes reasonably controlled)
PT/INR: 1.1

---

IMAGING REVIEWED (performed today):

Bone scan: Markedly progressive osseous disease. Multiple new areas of abnormal uptake compared to prior scan: left iliac crest (2 new foci), right proximal humerus (new), sacrum (new). The right femoral neck lesion, previously stable on enzalutamide restaging, now shows clearly increased uptake. Total osseous lesion count approximately 17 sites, compared to approximately 12 at the time enzalutamide was started. The previously responding lesions in T12 and left femoral head continue to show activity.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: The right obturator lymph node has grown to 2.1 cm (previously 0.9 cm on enzalutamide restaging 3 months ago). New retroperitoneal adenopathy: right para-aortic node 1.4 cm and aortocaval node 1.1 cm are new compared to prior study. Prostate/seminal vesicle bed shows stable post-treatment changes. Hepatic parenchyma appears homogeneous without discrete focal lesions. Lung windows: no pulmonary nodules or masses identified. No pleural effusion or ascites.

---

ASSESSMENT:

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old man with mCRPC, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, who has developed confirmed biochemical and radiographic progression on enzalutamide. He progressed following an approximately 8-month response (PSA nadir 1.4 ng/mL) with PSA now at 14.3 ng/mL, accompanied by new osseous lesions in the left iliac crest, right proximal humerus, and sacrum, worsening right femoral neck involvement, and new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Performance status has declined to ECOG 2, driven largely by pain and fatigue. There are no visceral metastases currently. He has no evidence of spinal cord compromise.

The genomic landscape of this patient's disease, including the confirmed germline BRCA2 p.E1308* with loss of heterozygosity in the tumor (a pathogenic truncating mutation leading to homologous recombination deficiency), strongly supports use of a PARP inhibitor. The PROfound trial demonstrated a significant progression-free and overall survival benefit for olaparib over physician's choice second-line NHA in patients with mCRPC and BRCA1/2 mutations who had progressed on a prior NHA (abiraterone or enzalutamide), and olaparib has FDA approval in this setting. Mr. Kowalski has now progressed on both abiraterone (first-line mHSPC) and enzalutamide (first-line mCRPC), and is therefore an ideal candidate for olaparib (Lynparza).

Symptom management: new left hip and shoulder pain attributable to radiographically confirmed lesions in the left iliac crest region and right proximal humerus respectively. Referral to radiation oncology for palliative EBRT to the right femoral neck lesion was completed at the prior visit, and he benefited significantly from that treatment. We will reassess whether additional palliative radiation is warranted to newly symptomatic sites.

Renal function has mildly worsened (eGFR ~42 mL/min); olaparib does not require dose adjustment until eGFR falls below 31 mL/min, so standard dosing of 300 mg BID is planned. Baseline cytopenias are present (Hgb 9.6 g/dL), and the risk of olaparib-associated myelosuppression, particularly anemia, was thoroughly discussed.

---

PLAN:

1. ENZALUTAMIDE: Discontinue enzalutamide effective today. No taper required.

2. OLAPARIB (Lynparza): Initiate olaparib tablets 300 mg orally twice daily. Indication: mCRPC with germline BRCA2 pathogenic mutation, progressed on prior NHA (abiraterone and enzalutamide), per PROfound trial data and FDA approval. Prior authorization submitted. Patient counseled extensively on:
   - Expected benefit: PSA response expected in approximately 60-70% of BRCA2-mutant mCRPC; improvement in pain and functional status possible.
   - Toxicities: myelosuppression (anemia most common, often requiring dose reduction or transfusion support), nausea and vomiting, fatigue, pneumonitis (rare), thromboembolic events. Risk of MDS/AML with prolonged use discussed.
   - Drug interactions: avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers. No significant interactions with current regimen.
   - Will need CBC monitoring every 4 weeks for the first year, then every 3 months.

3. ADT: Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months. Maintain castrate suppression. Next scheduled injection in 6 weeks.

4. BONE-DIRECTED THERAPY: Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks (dose-adjusted for CKD, eGFR ~42). Dental exam instructed; patient confirms no recent dental procedures planned. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.

5. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
   - Continue extended-release oxycodone 10 mg BID. Given new pain sites (left hip, shoulder), dose may require uptitration; patient advised to contact office if pain consistently above 5/10 or if analgesic requirements increase acutely.
   - Continue scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID.
   - Referral placed to palliative care team for co-management of pain and symptom burden.
   - Radiation oncology notified of new symptomatic lesions; will evaluate for palliative EBRT to left iliac crest/hip region if pain not controlled with systemic therapy.

6. MONITORING:
   - PSA, testosterone, CBC with differential, CMP (including creatinine, LFTs) in 4 weeks.
   - Restaging bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned after approximately 3 months on olaparib, or sooner if clinically indicated.
   - Close monitoring of hemoglobin given baseline anemia and PARP inhibitor-related myelosuppression risk.

7. RENAL FUNCTION: eGFR 42 mL/min. Continue metformin at current dose with cautious monitoring; hold if contrast dye required. Lisinopril and amlodipine continued for hypertension. Repeat creatinine at 4-week follow-up.

8. DIABETES: HbA1c 7.2%, reasonable control. Continue metformin. No change.

9. GENETIC COUNSELING: Previously completed. Relatives (children) advised on BRCA2 germline carrier implications.

10. GOALS OF CARE: Discussed. Patient's goals remain disease control with quality of life preservation. He wishes to remain at home and active as long as possible. Discussed realistic expectations regarding disease trajectory. Hospice was not raised as imminent at this visit, but was acknowledged as a future consideration depending on disease response. Wife present and engaged.

11. RETURN TO CLINIC: 4 weeks for labs and toxicity assessment on olaparib, or sooner PRN for pain escalation or new symptoms.

Time spent with patient and family in discussion of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment options: approximately 55 minutes.

All questions addressed. Patient verbalized understanding of new treatment plan and agreed to proceed.

[Dictated by: Attending Oncologist, GU Oncology]
[Reviewed and co-signed by: Fellow, GU Oncology]
